that her own demands were implicit in France's demand for the
Saar, threatened to quit the Conference unless Wilson gave
way. The French press began to raise a clamour; there had
been an outbreak of Bolshevism in Hungary, and soon, said the

20

Allies, the poison would spread to the West. Wilson was op-
posed even by his own advisers, Colonel House and Robert
Lansing. Though they were his good friends, they urgently
advised him, in view of the chaotic conditions that prevailed
in Europe, to sacrifice a few of his idealistic aims that the
other peace could be signed as speedily as possible. In fact
Wilson stood alone against a unanimously hostile front. From
America he was attacked in the rear by public opinion, which
was fanned by his political adversaries and rivals, and often
enough Wilson felt he had reached the end of his powers. He
admitted to a friend that he could not possibly continue to
hold his own against all the others, and said that he had de-
termined to leave the Conference unless he could carry his
point.
While thus engaged against such heavy odds, he was laid
low by an enemy from within. On April 3rd, when the fight
between crude realities and a still unattained ideal was near-
ing its climax, he was unable to keep upright any longer, and
--- a man of sixty-three --- had to take to his bed with influ-
enza. The onslaughts from the outer world were even more
formidable than those of his fevered blood, and gave him no
rest. Catastrophic tidings came to hand. On April 5th the
Communists rose to power in Bavaria, for a Soviet Republic
was established in Munich. At any moment Austria, hunger-
stricken and midway between a Bolshevik Bavaria and a Bolshevik

21

Austria, seemed likely to take the same course, and every
additional hour of resistance might make this lone fighter
Wilson responsible for the spread of red revolution. The
invalid's adversaries would leave him no peace in his sick-bed.
In the next room Clemenceau, Lloyd George, and Colonel House
were discussing matters, being all agreed that an end must be
reached at any cost. The cost would have to be paid by Wilson
with his demands and his ideals. His claim for a perpetual
peace should stand over, since it was
an obstacle to the more immediate need, that of an urgent
settlement of the "real", the military, the material peace.
But Wilson, exhausted, a sick man, irritated by the
clamour in the press which blamed him for blocking the way to
peace, forsaken by his own advisers, and deaved by the repre-
sentatives of the other governments, still would not give way.
He felt that he must keep his pledged word; that he would not
have done his utmost on behalf of the peace the others so
much wanted, unless he brought it into harmony with the non-
military, the lasting, the future peace, unless he continued
to do his utmost on behalf of the "world federation" which
was the only thing that could really establish the perpetual
peace of Europe. Hardly had he risen from his bed than he
took a decisive step. On April 7th he sent a cablegram to the
Navy Department in Washington: "What is the earliest possible

22

date U.S.S. 'George Washington' can sail for Brest France and
what is probable earliest date of arrival Brest? President
desires movements this vessel expedited". The same day the
world was informed that President Wilson had cabled for the
steamer by which he was to depart.
The news came like a thunderclap whose meaning was in-
stantly understood. All over the globe it became known that
President Wilson was determined to oppose any peace settlement
that should in the slightest degree infringe the principles
of the Covenant, and had resolved to quit the Conference rather
than to yield. A fateful hour had struck, one which would for
decades, for centuries, settle the destinies of Europe, of the
world at large. Should Wilson leave the Conference table, the
old order of society would collapse, chaos would begin --- but
perhaps it would be the chaos out of which a new star is born.
Europe looked on impatiently. Would the other members of the
Conference take such a responsibility? Would Wilson himself
take it? A fateful hour.
A fateful hour. At the moment Wilson was still firmly
resolved. He would not compromise; he would not yield; there
should not be "a hard peace", but "the just peace". The French
should not have the Saar; the Italians should not have Fiume;
Turkey should not be petitioned; there should be no "bartering
of peoples". Right should prevail over might, the ideal over
the real, the future over the present. Fiat justitia, pereat

23

mundus. This brief hour would be the greatest, the most per-
fectly human, the most heroic in Wilson's life. If he only
should have courage to stand firm, his name would be immortal-
ised among the rare true lovers of mankind, and he would have
done an unexampled deed. But the hour was followed by a week,
and during this week he was assailed from all sides. The
French, the British, the Italian press attacked him --- him,
the peace-maker --- for destroying the peace
by his theoretico-theological obstinacy, and for sacrificing
the real world to a private utopia. Even Germany, which had
looked to him as the main source of help, but had been alarmed
by the outbreak of Bolshevism in Bavaria, now turned against
him. So did his fellow-countrymen. Colonel House and Lansing
adjured him to give way. Even State Secretary Tumulty, who a
few days before had encouragingly cabled, "Only a bold stroke
by the President will save Europe and perhaps the world",
now, when Wilson was making the "bold stroke", was much per-
turbed, and cabled again, "Withdrawal most unwise and fraught
with most dangerous possibilities here and abroad .....
President should place the responsibility for a break of the
Conference where it properly belongs. .... A withdrawal at
this time would be a desertion".
Much perturbed, almost desperate, and with his self-
confidence impaired by the universality of dissent, Wilson
looked around. No one sided with him, everyone in the Confer-
ence hall was against him --- even the members of his own staff;

24

and the voices of the invisible millions upon millions, who
from a distance were imploring him to be firm, to abide by
his own principles, did not reach his ears. He never realised
that if he should act as he had threatened, and withdraw from
the Conference, his name would be immortalised; but that only
if he was steadfast would he bequeath his idea to the future
as a postulate to be perpetually renewed. He had no inkling
what creative energy would issue from his saying "No" to the
forces of greed, hatred, and unreason. All he could feel was
that he was alone, and that he was too weak to shoulder the
responsibility. The disastrous upshot was that President
Wilson became less stubbornly resistant, while Colonel House
built a bridge on which he could make compromises. The bar-
gaining about the frontiers went on for a week. At length,
on April 15, 1919 --- a black day in history --- with a heavy
heart and an uneasy conscience, Wilson agreed to the consider-
ably abated militarist demands of Clemenceau. The Saar was
not to become permanently French, but only for fifteen years.
The first compromise was made by the man who had hitherto been
uncompromising, and thereupon, as if a magician's wand had
been waved, the tone of the Parisian press was utterly differ-
ent next morning. The newspapers, which the day before had
railed at him as a disturber of the peace, as a man who was
ruining the world, extolled him as the wisest of living states-
men. But this praise seared him like a reproach. At the

25

bottom of his soul Wilson knew that though he had perhaps
saved the peace, the temporal peace, the permanent peace in
a spirit of reconciliation, the only peace that could save
the world, had been lost or thrown away. Folly had overcome
good sense, passion had prevailed against reason. Man had
been thrust back into an evil past. He, who had been the
leader and banner-bearer in the advance towards an ideal that
should transcend time, had lost the supreme battle, in which
he needed first of all to conquer his own weakness.
Did Wilson act rightly or wrongly in this fateful hour?
Who can tell? At any rate on this momentous and irrevocable
day he made a decision whose fruit will outlast decades and
centuries, and which we and our descendants will have to pay
for with our blood, our despair, our impotence, and our destruc-
tion. From this day Wilson's power, which had been morally
unrivalled, was broken, his prestige and energy were annulled.
He who makes one concession, cannot stop there. A compromise
inevitably leads to further compromises. Dishonour creates
dishonour, force begets force. Peace, which Wilson had visioned
as integral and lasting, remains fragmentary, transient, and
incomplete, because it was not fashioned in the sense of the
future, was not moulded out of the spirit of humanity, was not
constructed of the materials of pure reason. A unique oppor-
tunity, perhaps the most fateful in history, was pitifully

26

squandered, as the world, whose gods had been broken, soon
realised in the bitterness of disappointment and confusion.
When Wilson returned home, he who had been acclaimed as the
saviour of the world, was no longer regarded by anyone as a
redeemer. He was nothing more than a weary and elderly invalid,
doomed to a speedy death. Jubilation no longer greeted him,
nor did flags wave at his coming. As the ship steamed away
from the coast of Europe, he averted his face, for he could
not look back at our unhappy continent which for thousands
of years had longed for peace and unity, and had never found
them. Once again there vanished in the haze of distance the
everlasting dream of a humanised world.
WILSON'S FAILURE

objects.confidenceNotes: Die Transkription ist aufgrund der klaren Maschinenschrift sehr zuverlässig. Es handelt sich um zwei identische Kopien des Textes.

pages.transcription:
Buenos Aires 1936.

Dankbar sein ist leicht. Aber Danken ist eine grosse Kunst und
schwer wie jede Kunst zu meistern. Sie fordert eine Unbefangenheit
des Herzens, die ich in Eurem illustren Kreise nicht in mir finde,
denn ich fühle mich innerlich zu beunruhigt von der Frage, oh eine
Ehrung, wie sie die Academia de Brezil erwiesen hat, mir wirklich
gebürt. Ich gestehe Euch, meinen verehrten Collegen, diese Unsicher
heit ohne Scham ein, denn Ihr kennt sie gewiss selbst aus jenen tra
gischen Stunden des Zweifels, die wie schwarze Wolken oft tagelang
über unserer Arbeit schatten und uns die Herzen lähmen; wir wissen
überdies auch alle, wie arg wir unsere Leser meist enttäuschen, wenn
wir in persona vor ihnen erscheinen. Sie erwarten von uns eine fun
kelnde Meisterschaft der Rede, eine flirrende Fleurettkunst blen
dender Hiebe und des geschickten Ripostierens, sie vermuten bei je
dem von uns eine autoritäre Sicherheit der Haltung, ein - wie sie
in ihrer Güte meinen - berechtigtes Selbstgefühl. In Wirklichkeit
ist der Künstler in dem Maasse gehemmter und ungewisser, als er auf
richtig gegen sich selber ist; der Zweifel und die Unzufriedenheit
mit der eigenen Leistung sind beinahe sein normaler Seelenzustand.
Er fühlt sich befangen wenn andere von seinen Werken sprechen, weil
nur er allein den unsichtbaren Abstand von dem, was er erreichte,
kennt zu dem was er eigentlich wollte und sobald er sie mit den wirk
lich grossen Werken der Meister vergleicht ergreift ihn das Gefühl
seiner eigenen Unzulänglichkeit oft dermassen heftig, dass er in
einer heiligen Scham vor jedem Blick entfliehen möchte.

Gegen diese ständige und tragische Unsicherheit hält den Kün
stler nur eines aufrecht: die Bestätigung, die er von aussen erfährt
und vor allen jene, die von Männern stammt, die er selbst als Mei
ster und Kenner ehrt und verehrt. Es ist ein wunderbares Gefühl für
jeden Menschen dieser Erde, sich verstanden zu fühlen und sogar ge
liebt; jede Frau kennt dies Gefühl in ihrem Wesen, jeder Mann in sei
ner Leistung und wie keiner kommt es der schaffende Künstler, denn
pages.notes: Die erste Seite enthält handschriftliche Notizen am oberen Rand, die teilweise durchgestrichen sind.

pages.transcription:
- 2 -

Schaffen heisst doch im tiefsten; für andere schaffen, etwas aus
sich herausstellen; das nun anderen und möglichst allen gehören
soll. Es ist ein wunderbarer Augenblick, der uns für allen Zwei
fel den wir sonst erleiden, entschädigt, wenn wir sehen, dass et
was, was einst in uns lebte, nun aussen ein selbstständiges Leben
lebt, dass was früher uns bewegte, nun anderes fremdes Gefühl er
regt und wir über unsere eigene enge Existenz hinaus in einem Rau
me wirken, den wir selbst nicht mehr überschauen. Erlauben Sie mir
einen Augenblick lang von mir selbst zu sprechen und dankbar des
wunderbaren Erlebnisses zu gedenken, das mir bei Ihnen widerfah
ren ist. Brasilien war für mich von je eine magische Ferne. Als
Kind hatte ich stolz die schönen Briefmarken in mein Album geklebt
ich hatte als Knabe mit heissen Wangen von den Wundern des Amazo
nenstroms gelesen, ich hatte als Erwachsener immer Begeistertes
gehört von der Schönheit dieser Stadt, von der besonderen und un
vergleichlichen Cultur und Eigenart ihres Landes. Und in Jahren
und Jahren wuchs unbändiger mein Wunsch, die weite Reise zu wagen.
Endlich ward er erfüllt. Eine Woche glitt das Schiff über das rie
sige Meer und eine zweite, - beinahe ein Drittel des Erdballs
hatte ich durchmessen. Endlich war ich da - und nun erlebte ich
das Wunder: ein Teil von mir, lebendige abgesprengte Partikel mei
nes Wesens waren schon vor mir hier, ehe ich mit meinem eigenen
Körper diese neue Erde betrat. Meine Bücher, da waren sie, in an
derer Sprache und anderm Gewand, in den Auslagen der Buchhandlun
gen und mehr noch, tausendmal mehr: in den Herzen der Menschen.
Hier in dieser Stunde durfte ich an der liebevollen Rodo Musso
Leão mein eigenes Leben, all die gelebten Jahre, als eine Ein
heit erfahren. Ich erlebte mich vervielfach und gespiegelt, ich
genoss in wenigen Tagen soviel Güte und Freundschaft wie sonst in
Jahren. Wie sollte ich nicht restlos glücklich sein?

- 3 -

Und doch, meine verehrten Collegen, es liegt ein leiser
Schatten über meinem Glück. Ich fühle mich - warum nicht die
Wahrheit sagen vor Freunden? - in Eurem Kreise leise beschämt.
Ich fühle eine Schuld wider Euch - es ist nicht die meine, sondern
die grosse Schuld, die wir alle in Europa Euch gegenüber haben.
Ihr habt Euch wundervoll die Generosität des Herzens bewahrt.
Ihr nehmt auch in der Kunst den Fremden auf wie einen gern ge
sehenen Gast, ihr drängt ihn nicht beiseite, ihr seid noch nicht
von jener widerlichen Xenophobie, jener Fremdenfeindschaft und
Fremdenfurcht befleckt, die heute die Länder Europas so moralisch
hässlich macht. Und wir? Was wissen wir von Euch? Was haben wir
für Euch getan? Schmerzlich es zu sagen, aber ich wüsste in Euro
pa kaum jemanden, der mit gleicher Eindringlichkeit und Wissen
über einen von Euch sprechen könnte, wie es Musso Leão für mich
getan hat und, wichtiger noch, der es tuen wollte! Noch immer
ist bei uns der alto europäische Hochmut nicht abgetan, der alle
aussereuropäischen Länder gewissermassen als geistige Colonien
betrachtet, deren Huldigungen man gelassen hinnimmt, ohne daran
zu denken, sie würdig zu erwidern; noch immer kann und will man
nicht begreifen, dass die Weltuhr nicht im achtzehnten Jahrhundert
stehen geblieben ist und das Schwergewicht längst sich von Europa
weg verschoben hat. Meine verehrten Collegen und Freunde, ich
könnte es begreifen, wenn Euch manchmal eine geheime Bitterkeit
ergreift über diese Ungleichheit und Ungerechtigkeit, dass Ihr
immer die willig Gebenden gegen die fremden Künstler seid, dass
Ihr grossmütig die Werke der ganzen Welt in Eure Sprache und in
Eure Liebe nehmt, indes Eure Bücher, nicht geringer im Wert als
die unsern, uns, die wir Eurer Sprache nicht mächtig sind, nur
selten zugänglich werden! Ich könnte es verstehen, wenn Ihr Eure
Generosität schlecht entgolten fühlt, denn ich selbst fühle mich
von dieser Disparität so bedrückt, als hätte ich Teil an dieser

- 4 -

Schuld und was in meinen kleinen Kräften liegt, sie wettzumachen,
will ich von nun ab erfüllen wie eine Pflicht.

Ich sagte, meine verehrten Collegen, dass ich es mitfühlen
könnte, wenn Ihr manchmal mit Bitterniss empfindet, dass Eure
Wirkung bei gleicher Leistung so ungerechterweise in unserem Raum
verschlossen bleibt als die der andern Sprachen. Aber doch, welch
einen Stolz und welch ein Glücksgefühl müsst Ihr manchmal empfinden
gerade brasilianische Schriftsteller und Künstler zu sein! Denn
nichts Herrlicheres als einem Lande, in einem Volke, in einer
Sprache zu schaffen, das die Zukunft für sich hat! In jedem Augen
blicke um sich zu spüren, wie die Kraft, der Reichtum Eurer Nation
sich mehrt; ich hatte hier in Rio manchmal das Gefühl, wäre die
Brandung vor den Fenstern nicht so stark und würde es einen Augen
blick still, man könnte es geradezu hören, wie die Stadt wächst.
Für Euch, mit Euch schafft die Zeit! Für jede Tausend Leser werdet
ihr in einem Jahre schon zweitausend haben und in einem Jahre zehn
tausend, eine Zukunft ist Euch gegeben wie kaum einer Nation dieser
Welt und ihr werdet ihre Wegbereiter, ihre geistigen Führer, ihre
Erzieher gewesen sein. Wäre Neid nicht ein niederes Gefühl, dem je
der geistige Mensch sich ehern verschliessen muss, wir müssten Euch
beneiden um dies herrliche Bewusstsein, vom Schwung und Aufschwung
einer ganzen Nation einer wunderbaren Zukunft entgegengetragen zu
sein!

Aber noch ein besonderes ist Euch und gerade Euch gegeben in
der Kunst: ihr seid ein neues, ein junges Volk und habt als In
strument eine alte, eine erprobte, in Tradition gehärtete Sprache
und es kann nichts glücklicheres geben als diese Mischung. Ihr
müsst die Waffe, mit der ihr die ganze geistige Welt erobern wer
det, nicht erst schmieden, aber ihr werdet ihr einen neuen Schwung
und eine neue Schärfe geben. Eine Sprache ist ein Organismus von
ausserster Feinheit, er verändert sich bei jedem Druck der Luft;

- 5 -

alles was hier an Weiche, an Süsse, an hellerem und schärferen
Licht in der Atmosphäre liegt, was an Duft von den weiten Wäl
dern her schwebt, alles was an Geheimnis und Magie in Euren un
entdeckten Zonen waltet, wird schöpferisch eingehen in Euer Ge
dicht - einen anderen Rytmus werdet ihr Eurer Prosa geben als
die portugiesische Sprache ihn bislang kannte, denn immer weitet
sich die Seele eines Volkes einer Sprache an der Weite ihres Lands.
Auch ihr habt in der Kunst noch unermesslich viel unbekannten, un
verbrauchten Bodens vor Euch - nicht in alten Traditionen werdet
Ihr schaffen müssen, belastet von Erinnerungen, sondern eine ei
gene erschaffen. Wie viel liegt vor Euch! Wie voll ist dieses
Land noch von Romantik, wie lebendig und dynamisch erfüllt sich
Eure Geschichte, wie schön, wie einzig schön und in unerfassbarer
Vielfalt lebt Eure Landschaft, vergleichbar den herrlichsten auf
Erden - ja, Ihr habt noch, um ein commertielles Wort zu gebrauchen,
Rohstoff in Fülle, in einer Fülle sogar wie kein anderes Land die
ser Erde! Welche Lust hier an das Werk zu gehen! Fühlt mit ganzer
Seele, doch den Reichtum über Euch Kameraden, und gestaltet aus
dieser Fülle für Euch, für Eure Nation und für uns alle!

Diese Fülle, diese Kraft, diese Schönheit Eures Landes, die
besondere Güte und Gastlichkeit Eures Volkes, sie dringt auf jeden,
der hier rasten darf, unwiderstehlich ein - ich spreche die Wahr
heit, wenn ich sage, dass es schwer ist, hier nicht glücklich zu
sein. Schönheit erheitert die Sinne, Güte beseligt die Seele, hel
ler scheint dem Gast hier das Leben mit der helleren Sonne und
als ein grosses als ein unvergessliches Geschenk nimmt man die
Erinnerung an diese Tage mit. Vielleicht findet Ihr, dass ich Euch
nicht richtig und nicht genug gedankt habe, meine verehrten Kame
raden. Aber meine Worte sind nicht mein ganzer Dank. Ich will auf
richtig sein und Euch gestehen - ich will garnicht meine ganze

- 6 -

Dankbarkeit bei Euch in Eurem Lande lassen und dann zurückgehen,
leeren und vergesslichen Herzens. Nein, ich will diese Dankbarkeit
in mir bewahren, sie hüten und pflegen und weiss, sie wird
eine immer wachsende und fruchtbare sein. Und wenn ich mir vom
Leben noch etwas Schönes wünschen darf zu dem unerschöpflich Schö
nem, das ich hier gesehen und empfangen habe, so wäre es: - wieder
kehren zu dürfen in dieses wunderbare Land!

(Als Manuskript kopiert.

Rio de Janeiro, am 27. August 1936.)

Buenos Aires 1936.

objects.confidenceNotes: Der Text ist ein klares Typoskript. Die Lesbarkeit ist sehr hoch, abgesehen von den handschriftlichen Notizen auf der ersten Seite, die jedoch weitgehend entzifferbar sind.

pages.transcription:
Die Kunst ohne Sorgen zu leben
World copyright ~~Literary~~ Readers Digest New York
Es wäre undankbar, wenn ich den Mann ver-
gessen hätte, der mir zwei der schwersten Dinge auf
Erden gezeigt: wie man durch ~~eine~~ innere Souveräni-
tät sich der stärksten Macht dieser Erde, dem Geld,
entziehen kann, Und wie man {mitten unter Freunden} ~~einen Staat~~ 
zu leben vermag, ohne einen einzigen Feind zu haben.
Ich habe ~~ihn lange beobachtet und~~ {ehrlich} versucht, ihm die-
se seltene Meisterschaft abzulauschen. {aber} Ich gestehe,
dass es mir weder in dem einen noch in dem anderen
Fall bisher gelungen ist.
Ich lernte diesen einmaligen Menschen in
der kleinen Stadt, in der ich damals wohnte, auf die
einfachste Weise kennen. Ich führte eines Nachmit-
tags meinen treuen Spaniel, genannt Kaspar, spazie-
ren, der sonst übermütig mir vorauswedelte, diesmal
aber sich höchst närrisch benahm. Er wälzte sich mit
dem Rücken ingrimmig auf der Erde, die vier Beine in
der Luft, rieb sich an jedem Baum, heulte, murrte und
knurrte unablässig. Aber ich hielt dies für eine Un-
art des Frühlings und achtete nicht weiter darauf.
Mit einmal spürte ich, dass jemand mir zur
Seite ging. Ich blickte auf und sah ~~Schulter an~~ 
~~Schulter~~ einen etwa dreissigjährigen, ärmlich ge-
kleideten Mann ohne Hut, ohne Kragen {Schulter an Schulter mit mir} Schritt halten.
Ein Bettler, dachte ich unwillkürlich und wollte
schon in die Tasche greifen. Aber ruhig lächelte
aus blauen, klaren, hellbestirnten Augen dieser Frem-
de mir zu, als wären wir alte Freunde. "Er hat eine
Zecke, der arme Kerl", deutete er auf meinen Hund hin.
"Ich hab's gleich bemerkt. Komm, gehn wir hinüber
pages.notes: Die Transkription umfasst den Text auf Blatt 1. Handschriftliche Korrekturen wurden gemäß den Anweisungen integriert.

pages.transcription:
zur Bank drüben im Park. Ich krieg' sie schon heraus."
{nun} Ich muss hier bemerken, dass in der deutschen Sprache
sich nur jene Leute Du sagen, die gleichen Standes und
seit Jahren miteinander intim vertraut sind; sofern
ich also auf bürgerliche Sitte Gewicht gelegt hätte,
hätte ich diesen Unbekannten, der mich derart intim
ansprach, {groß/} abfertigen müssen. Aber in der Vertraulich-
keit seines Blicks lag eine solche Herzlichkeit {und}
Selbstverständlichkeit, dass sein Vorschlag auf mir
nicht ganz erklärliche Weise den Charakter eines Be-
fehls annahm. Ohne zu zögern, überquerte ich an sei-
ner Seite die Strasse. Wir setzten uns nebeneinan-
der auf die Bank. Er lockte mit einem scharfen Pfiff
den Hund heran.
Und sonderbar: mein Kaspar, der sonst je-
den neuen Menschen umständlich beschnuffelte, ehe er
sich von ihm berühren liess, legte ebenso gehorsam wie
ich selbst gerade gewesen, auf den ersten Wink dem
fremden Menschen den Kopf mit den schweren Ohren auf
die Knie. Der Unbekannte suchte mit seinen langen,
harten Fingern, an denen die Nägel merkwürdig hell
aufleuchteten, eine Zeitlang in dem Fell hin und her.
Dann klemmte er nach einem zufriedenen "aha" das ge-
duldige Tier zwischen die Knie und begann eine längere
Operation. Sie mochte schmerzhaft sein, denn Kaspar
wimmerte mehrmals leise, machte jedoch keinen Versuch,
sich loszureissen und hielt auf seinen krampfhaft ein-
gestemmten, nervös zitternden Beinen still.
Plötzlich liess ihn der Fremde aus dem
Schraubstock der Knie, lachte laut "Da haben wir's"
und hielt etwas triumphierend hoch. "Na, lauf los,
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 2 (im Dokument als Seite 2 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
Hunterl", rief er dem noch zitternden Tier zu, das
ihn sofort verstand und wie ein Kreisel loswirbelte.
Dann stand der Fremde auf, winkte mir "Grüss Gott" zu
und ging gleichmütig weiter mit sicherem Gang wie je-
mand, der seine Sache erledigt hat.
Ich war so verblüfft über dieses plötzliche
Fortgehen, dass es mir erst später einfiel, ich hätte
diesem sichtlich armen {für seine Mühe doch etwas} {furchtbar} {Menschen}
schenken müssen, etwas Geld oder ein paar Zigaretten,
zumindest {jedenfalls} hätte ich ihm ~~dazu~~ danken müssen.
Aber es war in dem Weggehen dieses Menschen {die}
gleiche abschliessende Sicherheit ~~und Selbstverständ-~~ 
~~lichkeit~~ gewesen wie in seinem Kommen. ~~Wäre sei-~~ 
~~ne Sache ganz sicher wollte, ob sie eine Freu-~~ 
~~dichten oder danken zu lassen, und ich wäre heute mit~~ 
~~grauen Haaren noch viel zu geben, wenn ich es er-~~ 
~~nen könnte, ein Gespräch mit einen Fremden so souverän~~ 
~~zu beginnen oder zu beenden.~~ Noch immer über die son-
derbare Art dieses Menschen nachdenkend, ging ich nach
Hause, und da in unserem Hause Kaspar die weitaus
wichtigste Person war, konnte ich nicht umhin, von
seinem Abenteuer zu berichten. Und sofort bemerkte
unsere alte Köchin mit begeisterter Selbstverständ-
lichkeit: "Ah, das war der Anton. Der sieht halt
alles."
Dadurch erfuhr ich, dass der hilfreiche
Samariter {den} Anton hiess und fragte nun weiter, {wie}
{er} nach seinem Berufe {sei} und was er eigentlich ma-
che. "Nichts", sagte unsere Köchin ärgerlich, als
hätte ich sie persönlich beleidigt, und fügte bei,
wie Frauen {nun} immer mit einer Frage antwortend:
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 3 (im Dokument als Seite 3 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
"Was braucht der einen Beruf?"
Um sie nicht herauszufordern, bemerkte
ich nur ganz vorsichtig, dass jeder Mensch doch von
irgend etwas leben müsse. "Ah, der braucht nix",
sagte sie stolz, "dem gibt jeder gern alles, was er
braucht." Nun musste ich beschämt die Tatsache ein-
gestehen, dass sich dieser sonderbare Anton gerade
von mir nichts hatte geben lassen. Aber das be-
stärkte unsere Köchin nur in ihrem Stolz. "Ja, der
bittet keinen um was. Der pfeift aufs Geld, der hat's
nicht notwendig."
Nun, das war sonderbar. Bisher hatte ich
immer geglaubt, dass jeder Mensch zum Leben Geld not-
wendig habe und wer keines besitze, sich mit irgend
einer bestimmten Art Arbeit verdienen müsse. Ich
wusste, dass man in dieser kleinen Stadt wie in jeder
anderen Stadt der Welt jedes Stück Brot und jedes Glas
Bier, seine Schlafstelle und seinen Rock bar bezahlen
musste, die Reichen manchmal erst nach einer Woche
oder einem Monat, die Armen sofort, ~~dass~~ {aus} hier wie
überall keinerlei Arbeit umsonst getan wurde, sondern
jede sogar nach einem bestimmten, von den Gewerkschaf-
ten genau festgesetzten Tarif. Wie machte es also der
kleine magere Bursche mit den durchgedrückten Hosen,
dies Gesetz zu durchbrechen? Wie entzog er sich dem
{feinmaschigen Netz} ~~darin~~ von Vereinbarungen und Tarifen, das heute un-
sere ganze Welt umspannt und war doch - dies hatte
ich gleich bei unserer ersten Begegnung bemerkt -
völlig sorglos und glücklich dabei. Ich beschloss,
seine besondere Methode auszukundschaften und musste
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 4 (im Dokument als Seite 4 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
schon nach wenigen Tagen unserer Köchin rechtgeben:
dieser Anton hatte in der Tat keinerlei bestimmten
Beruf, er ging wirklich den ganzen Tag spazieren.
Jeden Morgen zog er los und schlenderte durch die
Stadt, allerdings mit den wachsamsten, klügsten Augen,
die ein Mensch unter der Stirn haben kann. Er beobach-
tete alles und bemerkte alles. Er hielt einen Fuhrmann
an und machte ihn aufmerksam, dass das Pferd schlecht
gezäumt sei. Der lachte und liess es ihn besorgen,
weil es richtig war. Dann ging er an einem Zaun vor-
bei, winkte dem Besitzer und ermahnte ihn, das schon
morsche Holz streichen zu lassen; {nun} sofort betraute
der Gartenbesitzer ihn damit. Jeder liess ihn alles
tun, was er vorschlug, weil jeder wusste, dass {er} sich
{aus Geldgier} Anton keineswegs zudrängte, sondern nur uneigennützig und
ehrlich auf jeden Schaden hinwies. In wie vielen Be-
schäftigungen habe ich ihn aushelfend gesehen - einmal
traf ich ihn in einem Schusterladen sitzend und Schuhe
reparierend, einmal als Aushilfskellner bei einem Fest,
einmal Kinder spazierenführend, ein andermal auf dem
Markt mitten unter den Weibern Aepfel verkaufend: die
Frau lag im Wochenbette und so hatte sie - jeder wen-
dete sich in solchen Fällen immer an ihn - für die
paar Tage ihn als Aushilfe auf ihren Platz gesetzt.
Immerhin, solche fixe Burschen, die alles
können und zu allem zu brauchen sind, gibt es in vie-
len Exemplaren. Das Besondere und Einmalige aber an
diesem Anton war, dass er strikt ablehnte, sich {jemals} {auch für viele Sarten harter} 
mehr Geld geben zu lassen als er gerade für {arben} 
diese{n} einen Tages brauchte; {wenn} wenn es ihm gut ging,
{weigerte er sich stets eine Bezahlung} {immer überhaupt etwas} anzunehmen. "Ich komm
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 5 (im Dokument als Seite 5 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
schon später einmal zu dir, wenn ich was brauch",
sagte er in solchen Fällen. {Und bald ward ich gewahr:} Dieser hagere, meist
schlecht rasierte, zerschlissene Bursche hatte also
für seine Person ein neues, durchaus antikapitalisti-
sches System erfunden: er vertraute auf die Anstän-
digkeit der Menschen. Statt sich Geld {etwa} auf ein
Sparbuch zu deponieren, legte er sich lieber bei fast
allen Leuten dieser kleinen Stadt ein mobiles Kapital
{Er investierte sein bischer Besitz in unsichtbare} {Inklabon, dann moralische Verpflichtungen} von moralischen Verpflichtungen an; {selbst} der Hart-
näckigste und Misstrauischste konnte sich einem ge-
wissen Schuldnergefühl gegenüber {einem} {diesen} Menschen nicht
entziehen, der {nicht} mit seiner Arbeit, seinem Können
{nicht} Handel trieb, sondern alles, um das man ihn anging,
mit leichten und unbesorgten Händen tat, ohne soforti-
ge Entlohnung zu fordern. Man musste {Anton} {ihm} nur auf der
Strasse nachsehen, um zu merken, wie die Leute ihn {damit}
als etwas Besonderes respektierten; jeder grüsste ihn
herzlich, jeder schüttelte ihm die Hand, und nie habe
ich so gut die Macht eines Menschen verstanden, der
das grosse Lebensgeheimnis einfach im Blut hat, sich
vor dem Morgen nicht zu fürchten und wirklich, wie
man es in den Schulbüchern liest, auf Gott zu ver-
trauen. Sicherlich der Aermste von allen, ging die-
ser Nichtstuer und Allestuer, ging dieser einfache,
unbesorgte Mensch in seinem schlechten Rock durch die
kleine Stadt wie ein Gutsherr, der sein Gut inspiziert,
leutselig, freundlich; er konnte in jede Tür treten,
sich an jeden Tisch setzen, alles stand ihm offen,
weil er nichts wollte als das Notdürftigste. {Bald versteht es} {Das war}
{in letzten} das Geheimnis seiner Macht über die Men-
{sondern aus Menschlichkeit} {achtelen} schen - er diente nicht für Geld, und darum {dienten}
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 6 (im Dokument als Seite 6 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
{sie} ihm alle.
Nun muss ich offen eingestehen, dass es
{mich} {mir}, jetzt da ich ihn beobachtet hatte, {allmählich}
zu ärgern begann, dass er gerade an mir bloss mit
leichtem Gruss wie an einem halb Fremden vorüberging;
offenbar war er in seiner Feinfühligkeit der Meinung,
dass ich als Schriftsteller ihn zu nichts brauchen
konnte, und ich fühlte mich durch diese höfliche
Gleichgültigkeit irgendwie aus einer sehr grossen
und herzlichen Gemeinschaft ausgeschlossen. So be-
nützte ich die nächste Gelegenheit, als in unserem
Hause etwas nicht in Ordnung war - eine Dachrinne
liess Wasser durch - als Vorwand, um der Köchin vor-
zuschlagen, ob man nicht Anton die Sache zeigen solle.
Vielleicht könnte er herausbringen, wo der Fehler
steckte. Sie solle ihm schreiben oder zu ihm schicken.
"Zu dem kann man nicht schicken, der {schläft}
{wo nils gerade trifft} immer bei wem andern", antwortete sie. "Und schreiben
kann man ihm auch nicht. Aber ich lass ihm's schon
sagen." Wieder hatte ich etwas von dem sonderbaren
Menschen gelernt; er hatte als rechter Zugvogel kei-
ne Wohnung, keine Adresse. Und doch war niemand
leichter zu erreichen als er; eine Art drahtloser
Telephonie {die Verbindung} verband ihn mit der ganzen
Stadt. Es genügte, dem Erstbesten auf der Strasse zu
sagen: "Ich brauch den Anton", der sagte es wieder
dem Nächsten weiter und so fort, bis einer ihn traf.
Und tatsächlich, schon am Nachmittag war er da. Mit
seinen klugen, hellen Augen sah er alles im Hause an,
mahnte im Vorbeigehen durch den Garten, dass dort ein
Strauch geschnitten werden sollte, hier ein junger
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 7 (im Dokument als Seite 7 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
Baum umgesetzt. Wir führten ihn zum Dach, er unter-
suchte die Rinne, konstatierte nach paar Minuten "Hier
sitzt es" und begann schon zu arbeiten.
Es war eine Lust zu sehen, wie geschickt
er zupackte, aber leider hatte ich nicht Zeit, ihm
lange zuzusehen; Freunde aus Frankreich waren an-
gesagt, und nach zwei Stunden hatte ich den guten
Anton schon im Gespräch völlig vergessen. Da klopfte
{in unser Haus} es und er trat {herein} in Hendsärmeln, aber gar nicht
befangen vor den fremden Leuten. "Bleib nur sitzen!
Ich stör dich nicht", sagte er mit seiner freundlichen
Sicherheit und lächelte den fremden, eleganten Leuten
gutmütig zu, "ich wollt dir nur sagen, es ist schon
alles in Ordnung. Schau dir's nachher an. Grüss Gott!"
Wieder war er weg, ohne dass ich ihm hatte
{aber diesmal} {ist wenigstens} danken können. {Ich lief nur noch rasch} hinaus zur
Köchin, sie solle ihn gut bezahlen, denn ich selbst -
so stark war die Freiheit vom Geld, die von diesem
Menschen ausging - hätte nie den Mut gehabt, ihm wel-
ches anzubieten. Dann erst ging ich zu unseren Be-
suchern zurück. Aber nach paar Minuten überkam mich
die Unruhe, ob die Köchin ihn reichlich genug bezahlt
habe; ich liess sie kommen und fragte, ob er zufrie-
den gewesen sei. "Aber natürlich", antwortete sie.
"Der ist immer zufrieden. Sechs Schilling hab ich
ihm geben wollen, aber er hat nur zwei genommen. Das
reicht für heut und morgen, hat er gesagt. Aber wenn
der Herr Doktor einmal einen alten warmen Rock über-
flüssig hätt, das wär halt schön, hat er gesagt."
Ich kann schwer beschreiben, welche Art
plötzlicher Freude ich empfand, diesem ersten Menschen,
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 8 (im Dokument als Seite 8 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
der weniger nahm, als man ihm gab, {anbieten} {und doch etwas} 
{geben} zu dürfen, was er sich innerlich wünschte. {Diese Pflicht} {Es}
schien mir wichtiger als alle Höflichkeit. Ziem-
lich rücksichtslos liess ich meine Gäste im Stich
und rannte ihm nach bis zum Gartentor. "Anton! Anton!
Ich gebe Ihnen gleich einen Rock mit." Wieder kam mir
das heitere, ruhige Licht seiner Augen entgegen. Er
war nicht im mindesten erstaunt, dass ich ihm nachge-
laufen war. In seiner Weltauffassung war es vollkom-
men selbstverständlich, dass jemand, der einen über-
flüssigen Rock besitzt, ihn dem andern anbietet, der
gern einen haben möchte. Gelassen folgte er mir {nach}
und eilig liess ich von alten Kleidern, Schuhen, Hem-
den alles Erreichbare zusammenholen. Er sah den gan-
zen aufgehäuften Packen sorgfältig mit seinem sachli-
chen Blick an, nahm den Rock, probierte ihn an und
sagte dann ruhig: "Ja, den kann ich brauchen." - Er
sagte es absolut in dem Ton, wie eine Millionärin
beim Juwelier einen Ring sich auswählt. Er sagte
es nicht wie einer, der eine Sache geschenkt bekommt,
sondern mit der Souveränität eines Herrn im Kaufladen,
der ohne nach dem Preis zu fragen, von den vorgelegten
Stücken sich zu einem entschliesst. "Ja, der ist
schön", wiederholte er zufrieden, dann blickte er mit
sinnendem Blick auf die anderen Sachen. "Die Berg-
schuh dorten gibt dem Fritz in der Salsergassen, der
braucht welche, und die Hemden dem Josef vom Platzl,
der kann sie sich selber flicken. Wenn du willst,
bring ich sie dir hin" - wieder sagte er es im gross-
{so} {benah} mütigen Ton eines Angebots, dass ich {eigentlich} das
Gefühl hatte, ich müsste ihm noch danken dafür, dass
pages.notes: Transkription von Blatt 9 (im Dokument als Seite 9 bezeichnet).

pages.transcription:
er die mir gehörigen Sachen an mir völlig fremde Per-
sonen verteilte. Sorgfältig knotete er die Dinge in
einen Packen und lud ihn auf die Schulter. Dann sag-
te er noch herzlich herablassend: "Ja, du bist ein
braver Kerl. Schön von dir, dass'd das alles her-
gibst" und war verschwunden.
Sonderbar, {denn begeisterte Darstellung irgend etwas} {wenig} hat mich dermassen ge-{freut} {Bücher}
freut in meinem Leben wie die naive Art des Lobes.
Ich habe oft noch an diesen Anton gedacht und immer
{voll dank} {dankbar}, denn wenige Menschen haben mir innerlich so
sehr geholfen. Oft wenn ich mir kleine, dumme Sor-
gen um Geld machte, erinnerte ich mich an diesen Men-
schen, der ruhig und vertrauensvoll in den Tag lebte,
weil er nicht mehr wollte als genug für diesen einen
Tag. Wie oft habe ich ihn noch {in seinem armen Anfang} {aber} gesehen, {nie anders}
als heiter und unbesorgt, und immer mir dann gedacht:
wenn alle Menschen untereinander dies Geheimnis der
Reziprozität des Vertrauens lernten, müsste es keine
Polizei geben, keine Gerichte, keine Gefängnisse und
kein Geld. Unser ganzes kompliziertes ökonomisches
System, das sich immer wieder selbst ad absurdum
führt und zum "sozialen Problem" wird, wäre vielleicht
gelöst, wenn alle lebten wie dieser Eine, der nur ge-
nau so viel nahm als er brauchte und so viel von sich
gab als er vermochte. Jetzt weiss ich schon seit Jah-
ren nichts mehr von ihm. Aber um wenige Menschen habe
ich so wenig Sorge als um ihn: ich weiss, diesen Mann
wird Gott und, was seltener ist, ihn werden sogar die
Menschen nicht im Stich lassen.

pages.transcription:
THE MAN WITH THE SECRET OF HAPPINESS
World Copyright {Readers} Digest, New York 1939
I should be ungrateful indeed had I forgotten the
person who showed me two of the most difficult things on
earth: how, by means of ~~the sovereignty of the mind~~ {an inner freedom}, a man
can free himself from the strongest power in this world,
the power of money; and how a man can live among his fellow
human beings without making a single enemy. I have honestly
tried to learn {from him} this rare art, but I must confess
that hitherto I have succeeded neither in the one respect
nor the other.
It was in a very simple way that I came to {unique} know this
singular individual in the little town where I was living
at the time. One afternoon I was taking my faithful
spaniel for a walk. Kaspar, who as a rule frisked along
ahead of me, on this occasion behaved in an extremely foolish
manner. He lay on his back and rolled frenziedly on the
ground, all four feet in the air, rubbed himself against
every tree, whimpered, ~~howled~~ and growled incessantly. But
I took this to be spring-time naughtiness and paid no further
heed to it.
All of a sudden I became aware that someone was walking
by my side. Looking up, I saw that a man of about thirty,
poorly dressed, collarless and hatless, was keeping pace
with me. A beggar, I thought involuntarily, and was about
to put my hand in my pocket. But the stranger smiled
tranquilly at me out of clear starry blue eyes as though we
were old friends. "He's got a tick, poor chap," he said,
pointing to my dog. "I could tell that at once. Come
along, we'll go over to that bench in the park, and I'll
have it out in no time."
I must remark at this point that {in adressing {he used „du“}} ~~he addressed me in the~~ 
second person singular, which in German is only employed
among people {are} ~~of the same class~~ who have been on intimate
pages.notes: Die Transkription umfasst die handschriftlichen Korrekturen und Einfügungen über den Zeilen.

pages.transcription:
-2-
terms {for years}; if, therefore, I had attached great
importance to the ordinary conventions I should inevitably
have sent this stranger {who addressed me thus familiarly} {too-familiar} {but} 
about his business. But there was such warm friendliness
in {the familiarity of} his gaze that in some almost indefinable
way his suggestion assumed for me the nature of a command.
Without a moment's hesitation I crossed the street with him
and we sat down side by side on the bench. He called the
dog {to him} with a shrill whistle.
And strange to say, my Kaspar, who usually sniffed and
snuffled round every stranger and made a great to-do before
letting himself be touched, responded as obediently as I
had {myself} {just done} and at a sign from {the stranger} {The man} placed his
head with its heavy ears on his knee. {The unknown man} {He} 
searched about in {the dog's} {his} coat for a time with long, horny
fingers, {the nails of which} {wore} shone with remarkable brightness.
Then, with a satisfied "Aha!" he gripped the patient animal
between his knees and began a lengthy operation. It must
have been painful, for Kaspar whimpered faintly several
times, but stood still on tautened, trembling legs, and made
no effort to wriggle free.
Suddenly the stranger released him from the vice-like
grip of his knees. "Here it is," he laughed, triumphantly
holding something in the air. "Now run along, doggie,"
he said to the still trembling animal, who understood {him}
at once and whirled off like a top. Then the stranger
rose, and with a nod and a "Grüss Gott," walked coolly on
with the sure step of a man who has made a good job of
something.
I was so taken aback by his sudden departure that it
only later occurred to me that I really ought to have given
the fellow, {who was quite obviously wretchedly poor} {poor}, something
for his trouble - some money or a few cigarettes. At any
rate I ought at least to have thanked him. But there was
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die zahlreichen handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-3-
the same finality and self-possession about his going as
his coming. Still pondering on the odd behaviour {of this} {man's} 
stranger, I walked home, and, {as} {since} Kaspar was by far the
most important personage in our household, I could not
refrain from reporting his adventure. Whereupon our old
cook remarked with eager matter-of-factness: "Oh, that
was Anton. He's got an eye for everything."
It was thus I learned that the good Samaritan was
called Anton, and I went on to ask the cook what was his
trade and what he actually did. "Nothing," said our cook
peevishly, as though she regarded my question as a personal
insult, and then, answering, as women will, with a question,
added: "What does he want with a trade?"
Anxious not to provoke her, I merely remarked {very}
tentatively that everyone had to have something to live on.
"Oh, he doesn't need anything," she said proudly. "Everyone
is only too glad to give him whatever he wants." {And now,}
{I was obliged} {I admitted} {shame-facedly} {to confess} that from me at least
this {strange} {queer} Anton had asked for nothing. But this only
fortified our cook in her pride. "Yes, he never asks
anyone for anything. He doesn't care a straw about money,
he doesn't need it."
Well, this was odd. Hitherto I had always believed
that everyone needed money in order to live, and that
{anyone} {more} who had none must earn a living by doing some kind
of work. I knew that in our little town, as in every other
town in the world, every crust of bread and every glass of
beer, a lodging for the night and a coat to wear, had to be
paid in cash, by the rich sometimes at the end of the
week or the month, by the poor on the spot; that here as
everywhere no work of any kind was done for nothing, indeed
that all work was performed for a fixed trade union rate of
pay.
How came this spare little fellow, then, with the
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-4-
threadbare trousers, to get round this law? How was it
that he eluded the finely-meshed net of agreements and rates
which to-day is spread over the whole of our world, and was
utterly carefree and happy? I resolved to investigate his
special technique and was {very} soon obliged to admit that
our cook had been right: this fellow Anton had no kind of
settled job, he really did wander about all day long.
Every morning he set out and strolled through the town, with
the shrewdest, most watchful pair of eyes, to be sure, a
man could possibly have in his head. He observed everything
and noticed {everything} {all shortcomings}. He would stop the driver of a cart
and draw his attention to the fact that his horse was {imperfectly} {badly}
harnessed. The man would laugh and attend to the matter.
{For Anton had been quite correct.} Then Anton would walk
past a fence, beckon to the owner of the garden and advise
him to have the rotting wood {newly} painted, and the latter
would {entrust} {ask} {him} {to do} {with} the job. Everyone let him do whatever
he suggested, because everyone knew that Anton never intruded
out of cupidity, but merely disinterestedly and sincerely
drew people's attention to {anything that was} {things} out of order.
How many jobs have I not seen him putting his hand to!
Once I found him sitting in a shoemaker's mending shoes,
once acting as an {extra} additional waiter at a party, once taking
some children out for a walk, another time selling apples
among the market-women; the owner of one of the stalls was
in child-bed, and so - everyone turned to him {in an} {on such} 
emergency {occasions} - she had got him to take her place for a few days.
All the same, there are plenty of such handy fellows
who can turn a hand to anything {and are always ready to} 
help. The special and unique thing about Anton, however,
was that he firmly refused to take more money, even for many
hours of hard work, than precisely the {amount} {wear} he needed for
{that} {the} day; and when things {were going well with him} {went} he flatly
declined to accept any payment whatever. "I'll come to you
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-5-
later on if I need anything," he would say in such cases.
And I soon became aware that this gaunt, {usually ill-shaven}
ragged fellow had discovered for himself a new and
fundamentally anti-capitalist system; he had faith in the
decency of human beings. Instead of depositing money in
a saving's bank, he preferred to accumulate {easily realisable}
{capital in the form of} moral obligations with almost all the
people in the little town; he invested his little {fortune} {all} in
invisible credits, for even the most obstinate and mis-
trustful could not escape a certain feeling of indebtedness
towards a person who did not make a business of his work {and}
his capacities, but did anything that was asked of him {as}
{though it were the most natural thing in the world,} {by way of favour} without
{demanding immediate payment.} {Thought of fixed compensation} One only had to gaze after
Anton in the street to realise in what special esteem people
held him for this; everyone had a cordial greeting for
him, everyone shook him by the hand, and never have I
understood so well the power wielded by a man who has the
great secret of life in his blood, the secret of taking no
thought for the morrow, and of genuinely, as the school-books
say, trusting in God. {Quite certainly the poorest of the} 
{poor} This "Do-nothing" and yet "Do-all", this simple
carefree man in the shabby coat, walked through the town
like a landowner inspecting his estates, with a genial and
friendly air; he could enter every door, sit down at every
table, everything was his to command, because he never wanted
anything but the barest necessities. Soon I came to under-
stand the secret of his power over people - he served not for
money but out of sheer human kindness, and it was on that
account that everyone respected him.
I must frankly admit that {since I had begun to observe} {notice}
him, it gradually began to annoy me to {find} that he passed me
of all people merely with a casual greeting as though I were
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-6-
more or less a stranger. Evidently his delicacy of feeling
was such as to {led} {help} him to assume that I, as a writer, could
not possibly require his services; and I felt myself in
some way or other excluded by this polite indifference from
a large and friendly community. And so the {very} next time
something was out of order in our house - water was dripping
from a gutter - I seized the opportunity to suggest to my
cook that Anton should be asked to have a look at it;
perhaps he might be able to discover what had gone wrong;
she was to write to him or send for him.
"You can't send for him, he is always staying with
someone different - {wherever he happens to be at the moment,}" 
she replied. "And you can't write to him, either. But
I'll get word to him somehow." Once more I had learned
something fresh about this strange individual: a veritable
bird of passage, he had no home, no permanent address. And
yet no one was easier to get in touch with than he; a sort
of wireless telephony connected him with the whole town. It
was sufficient to tell the first person you met in the
street, "I want Anton," and he would tell the next person he
met, and so it would go on until someone ran across him. And
indeed that very afternoon he turned up. He looked at
everything in the house with his shrewd, bright eyes, pointing
out, as he walked through the garden, that here a bush wanted
{trimming} {cutting down}, there a young tree transplanting. We took
him up to the roof, where he inspected the gutter. "This is
where the trouble is," he announced after a few minutes, and
set to work forthwith.
It was a pleasure to see how skilfully he tackled the job,
but unfortunately I had no time to watch him for very long;
I was expecting some friends from France, and after two hours
in their company I had forgotten all about the good Anton.
Then there was a knock at the door and he entered the room
in his shirt-sleeves, not in the least abashed at the sight
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-7-
{“Don’t bother”} of my visitors. "Stay where you are, I shan't disturb
you," he said in his genial, self-assured way, smiling
good-naturedly at the {eager} {well-dressed} strangers. "I
only wanted to tell you everything's all right now. You
go and have a look later on. Grüss Gott."
Again he had {left} {gone} without my having had a chance of
thanking him. But this time at least I rushed off to tell
the cook to pay him well, for I myself - so strongly was one
conscious of this man's complete independence of money -
{should} {not} {never} {have} had the courage to offer him anything.
Only after having done this did I return to my visitors. But
in a few minutes I began to wonder uneasily whether the cook
had paid him handsomely enough. I sent for her and asked her
if he had been satisfied. "Well, of course," she said.
"He's always satisfied. I wanted to give him six Schillings,
but he would only take two. That would see him through for
to-day and to-morrow, he said. But if the Herr Doktor, he
said, ever had an old winter coat to spare, that would be
very nice."
I find it hard to describe the shock of pleasure it gave
{at last} me to be able to offer to this, the first person I had ever
come across who took less than was given to him, something
that he was {eager} anxious to have. This was a duty that seemed to
me more important than that of a host. Somewhat inconsiderately
I abandoned my guests and ran after him to the garden gate.
{“I called down the hill”} "Anton, Anton, I'll give you a coat now to take away with {you} {your}
your." Once more I encountered that serene, tranquil light
in his eyes. He was not in the least surprised that I should
have run after him. Nothing could be more natural to him,
with his philosophy, than that {someone} {he} who had a coat he did
not need should offer it to {another} someone else who badly wanted
one. He followed me calmly and I hurriedly got the cook to
fetch all my available old clothes, shoes and shirts. He
carefully scrutinised the great pile with his expert eye,
pages.notes: Die Transkription enthält die handschriftlichen Korrekturen.

pages.transcription:
-8-
picked up the coat, tried it on and then said quietly:
"Yes, this will do me." He said this in the tone of a
millionaire's wife choosing a ring at a jeweller's. He
said it not with the air of {receiving} someone being given a present
but in the {restrained} {lofty} tone of a gentleman who, without asking
the price, decides to take one of the articles brought out
for his inspection in a shop. "Yes, it's very nice," he
said contentedly, then glanced contemplatively at the other
things. "You can give those walking shoes to Fritz in the
Salsergasse, he needs a pair, and the shirts to Josef in the
square, he can patch them for himself. If you like I'll
{uttered} take them along for you." This again he said in the
magnanimous tone of one volunteering to do a favour, and I
almost had a feeling that I ought to thank him for distributing
my belongings among people who were complete strangers to me.
Carefully tying up the things into a bundle, he lifted them
on to his shoulder. Then he added in tones of kindly
condescension, "Yes, you're a good fellow. Nice of you to
give all these things away," and vanished.
Strange, no enthusiastic review of any of my books had
ever delighted me so much as this naive praise. I have
often thought of {this} Anton, and always with gratitude, for few

when I have been worrying about stupid little money matters
I have called to mind this man who lived calmly and confidently

clothes, but never otherwise than serene and carefree! And
always I have thought to myself: if everyone in the world
were to learn this secret of mutual trust and confidence,
there would be no police, no courts of law, no prisons and
no money. Our entire complicated economic system, which
reduces itself ever and again to an absurdity and has become

pages.transcription:
Digest, New York
1939
THE MAN WITH THE SECRET OF HAPPINESS
by Stefan Zweig, 49 Hallam Street
London W1

I should be ungrateful indeed had I forgotten the
person who showed me two of the most difficult things on
earth: how, by means of {an inner freedom,} the ~~sovereignty of the mind,~~ a man
can free himself from the strongest power in this world,
the power of money; and how a man can live among his fellow
human beings without making a single enemy. I have honestly
tried to learn from him this rare art, but I must confess
that hitherto I have succeeded neither in the one respect
nor the other.

It was in a very simple way that I came to know this
{unique} ~~singular~~ individual in the little town where I was living
at the time. One afternoon I was taking my faithful
spaniel for a walk. Kaspar, who as a rule frisked along
ahead of me, on this occasion behaved in an extremely foolish
manner. He lay on his back and rolled frenziedly on the
ground, all four feet in the air, rubbed himself against
every tree, whimpered, ~~[...4...]~~ and growled incessantly. But
I took this to be spring-time naughtiness and paid no further
heed to it.

All of a sudden I became aware that someone was walking
by my side. Looking up, I saw that a man of about thirty,
poorly dressed, collarless and hatless, was keeping pace
with me. A beggar, I thought involuntarily, and was about
to put my hand in my pocket. But the stranger smiled
tranquilly at me out of clear starry blue eyes as though we
were old friends. "He's got a tick, poor chap," he said,
pointing to my dog. "I could tell that at once. Come
along, we'll go over to that bench in the park, and I'll
have it out in no time."
I must remark at this point that {in addressing me he used „Du“, the} he ~~addressed me in the~~
second person singular, which in German is only employed
among people ~~of the same class who have been~~ {who are} on intimate
pages.notes: Die Seite enthält handschriftliche Korrekturen und Einfügungen. Der Text ist teilweise durchgestrichen.

pages.transcription: blank

--2--
terms {for years;} if, therefore, I had attached great
importance to the ordinary conventions I should inevitably
have sent this stranger who {had} ~~too familiar~~ addressed me thus familiarly
about his business. But there was such warm friendliness
in the ~~familiarity of~~ his gaze that in some almost indefinable
way his suggestion assumed for me the nature of a command.
Without a moment's hesitation I crossed the street with him
and we sat down side by side on the bench. He called the
dog {to him} with a shrill whistle.

And strange to say, my Kaspar, who usually sniffed and
snuffled round every stranger and made a great to-do before
letting himself be touched, responded as obediently as I
had {myself} ~~just done~~ and at a sign from the {man} ~~stranger~~ placed his
head with its heavy ears on his knee. {The unknown man}
He searched about in {the dog's} his coat for a time with long, horny
fingers, {the nails of which} ~~the nails~~ shone with remarkable brightness.
Then, with a satisfied "Aha!" he gripped the patient animal
between his knees and began a lengthy operation. It must
have been painful, for Kaspar whimpered faintly several
times, but stood still on tautened, trembling legs, and made
no effort to wriggle free.

Suddenly the stranger released him from the vice-like
grip of his knees. "Here it is," he laughed, triumphantly
holding something in the air. "Now run along, doggie,"
he said to the still trembling animal, who understood {him}
at once and whirled off like a top. Then the stranger
rose, and with a nod and a "Grüss Gott," walked coolly on
with the sure step of a man who has made a good job of
something.

I was so taken aback by his sudden departure that it
only later occurred to me that I really ought to have given
the {poor} fellow, who ~~was quite obviously wretchedly poor,~~ something
for his trouble - some money or a few cigarettes. At any
rate I ought to at least have thanked him. But there was
pages.notes: Seite 2 des Textes (paginiert als --2--). Enthält zahlreiche Korrekturen.

pages.transcription: blank

--3--
the same finality and self-possession about his going as
his coming. Still pondering on the {man's} odd behaviour of this
~~stranger,~~ I walked home, and {as} since Kaspar was by far the
most important personage in our household, I could not
refrain from reporting his adventure. Whereupon our old
cook remarked with eager matter-of-factness: "Oh, that
was Anton. He's got an eye for everything."

It was thus I learned that the good Samaritan was
called Anton, and I went on to ask the cook what was his
trade and what he actually did. "Nothing," said our cook
peevishly, as though she regarded my question as a personal
insult, and then, answering, as women will, with a question,
added: "What does he want with a trade?"

Anxious not to provoke her, I merely remarked {very}
tentatively that everyone had to have something to live on.
"Oh, he doesn't need anything," she said proudly. "Everyone
is only too glad to give him whatever he wants." {I admitted}
{I was} ~~I was~~ ~~obliged~~ shame-facedly to confess that from me at least
this {queer} ~~stranger~~ Anton had asked for nothing. But this only
fortified our cook in her pride. "Yes, he never asks
anyone for anything. He doesn't care a straw about money,
he doesn't need it."

Well, this was odd. Hitherto I had always believed
that everyone needed money in order to live, and that
{those} ~~anyone~~ who had none must earn a living by doing some kind
of work. I knew that in our little town, as in every other
town in the world, every crust of bread and every glass of
beer, a lodging for the night and a coat to wear, had to be
paid for in cash, by the rich sometimes at the end of the
week or the month, by the poor on the spot; that here as
everywhere no work of any kind was done for nothing, indeed
that all work was performed for a fixed trade union rate of
pay.

How came this spare little fellow, then, with the
blank

--4--
threadbare trousers, to get round this law? How was it
that he eluded the finely-meshed net of agreements and rates
which to-day is spread over the whole of our world, and was
utterly carefree and happy? I resolved to investigate his
special technique and was ~~soon~~ obliged to admit that
our cook had been right: this fellow Anton had no kind of
settled job, he really did wander about all day long.
Every morning he set out and strolled through the town, with
the shrewdest, most watchful pair of eyes, to be sure, a
man could possibly have in his head. He observed everything
and noticed {all shortcomings} ~~everything~~. He would stop the driver of a cart
and draw his attention to the fact that his horse was {imperfectly} ~~badly~~
harnessed. The man would laugh and attend to the matter,
{for Anton had been quite correct.} Then Anton would walk
past a fence, beckon to the owner of the garden and advise
him to have the rotting wood {painted,} ~~painted,~~ and the latter
would {ask him to do} ~~entrust him with~~ the job. Everyone let him do whatever
he suggested, because everyone knew that Anton never intruded
out of cupidity, but merely disinterestedly and sincerely
drew people's attention to {things} ~~anything~~ that was out of order.
How many jobs have I not seen him putting his hand to!

Once I found him sitting in a shoemaker's mending shoes,
{extra} once acting as an additional waiter at a party, once taking
some children out for a walk, another time selling apples
among the market-women; the owner of one of the stalls was
in child-bed, and so - everyone turned to him {in an emergency}
~~occasiona~~ - she had got him to take her place for a few days.

All the same, there are plenty of such handy fellows
who can turn a hand to anything, and are always ready to
help. The special and unique thing about Anton, however,
was that he firmly refused to take more money, even for many
hours of hard work, than precisely {what} the amount he needed for
{that} ~~one day;~~ {went well} and when things {were going well with him} he flatly
declined to accept any payment whatever. "I'll come to you
blank

--5--
later on if I need anything," he would say in such cases.
And I soon became aware that this gaunt, {usually} ill-shaven
ragged fellow had discovered for himself a new and
fundamentally anti-capitalist system; he had faith in the
decency of human beings. Instead of depositing money in
a saving's bank, he preferred to accumulate {easily realisable}
capital in the form of moral obligations with almost all the
people in the little town; he invested his little {fortune} in
invisible credits, for even the most obstinate and mis-
trustful could not escape a certain feeling of indebtedness
{to} towards a person who did not make a business of his work and
his capacities, but did anything that was asked of him {as}
though it were {the most natural thing in the world,} ~~by way of favour,~~ without
{without thought of fixed compensation.} demanding immediate payment. (One only had to gaze after
Anton in the street to realise in what special esteem people
held him for this; everyone had a cordial greeting for
him, everyone shook him by the hand, and never have I
understood so well the power wielded by a man who has the
great secret of life in his blood, the secret of taking no
thought for the morrow, and of genuinely, as the school-books
say, trusting in God. {This "Do-nothing" and yet "Do-all", this simple}
carefree man in the shabby coat, walked through the town
like a landowner inspecting his estates, with a genial and
friendly air; he could enter every door, sit down at every
table, everything was his to command, because he never wanted
anything but the barest necessities. Soon I came to under-
stand the secret of his power over people - he served not for
money but out of sheer human kindness, and it was on that
account that everyone respected him.

I must frankly admit that {notice} ~~it gradually began to annoy me to~~ {that} he passed me
of all people merely with a casual greeting as though I were
blank

--6--
more or less a stranger. Evidently his delicacy of feeling
{led} him to assume that I, as a writer, could
not possibly require his services; and I felt myself in
some way or other excluded by this polite indifference from
a large and friendly community. And so the {next} time
something was out of order in our house - water was dripping
from a gutter - I seized the opportunity to suggest to my
cook that Anton should be asked to have a look at it;
perhaps he might be able to discover what had gone wrong;
she was to write to him or send for him.

"You can't send for him, he is always staying with
someone different" - she replied. "And you can't write to him, either. But
I'll get word to him somehow." Once more I had learned
something fresh about this strange individual: a veritable
bird of passage, he had no home, no permanent address. And
yet no one was easier to get in touch with than he; a sort
of wireless telephony connected him with the whole town. It
was sufficient to tell the first person you met in the
street, "I want Anton," and he would tell the next person he
met, and so it would go on until someone ran across him. And
indeed that very afternoon he turned up. He looked at
everything in the house with his shrewd, bright eyes, pointing
out, as he walked through the garden, that here a bush wanted
{trimming,} there a young tree transplanting. We took
him up to the roof, where he inspected the gutter. "This is
where the trouble is," he announced after a few minutes, and
set to work forthwith.

It was a pleasure to see how skilfully he tackled the job,
but unfortunately I had no time to watch him for very long;
I was expecting some friends from France, and after two hours
in their company I had forgotten all about the good Anton.
Then there was a knock at the door and he entered the room
in his shirt-sleeves, not in the least abashed at the sight
blank

--7--
of my visitors. "Don't bother, I shan't disturb
you," he said in his genial, self-assured way, smiling
good-naturedly at the well-dressed strangers. "I
only wanted to tell you everything's all right now. You
go and have a look later on. Grüss Gott."

Again he had {left} without ~~thanking him.~~ But this time at least I rushed off to tell
the cook to pay him well, for I myself - so strongly was one
conscious of this man's complete independence of money -
{not} had the courage to offer him anything. {Then} I return to my visitors. But
in a few minutes I began to wonder uneasily whether the cook
had paid him handsomely enough. I sent for her and asked her
if he had been satisfied. "Well, of course," she said.
"He's always satisfied. I wanted to give him six Schillings,
but he would only take two. That would see him through for
to-day and to-morrow, he said. But if the Herr Doktor, he
said, ever had an old winter coat to spare, that would be
very nice."

I find it hard to describe the shock of pleasure it gave
{at last} me to be able to offer to this, the first person I had ever
come across who took less than was given to him, something
{eager} that he was to have. This was a duty that seemed to
me more important than that of a host. Somewhat inconsiderately
I abandoned my guests and ran after him to the garden gate.
"Anton, Anton," {I called down the hill, "I have a coat for you now."
Once more I encountered that serene, tranquil light
in his eyes. He was not in the least surprised that I should
have run after him. Nothing could be more natural to him,
with his philosophy, than that someone who had a coat he did
not need should offer it to {another} someone else who badly wanted
one. He followed me calmly and I hurriedly got the cook to
fetch all my available old clothes, shoes and shirts. He
carefully scrutinised the great pile with his expert eye,
blank

--8--
picked up the coat, tried it on and then said quietly:
"Yes, this will do me." He said this in the tone of a
millionaire's wife choosing a ring at a jeweller's. He
said it not with the air of {one receiving} a present
but in the {restrained} tone of a gentleman who, without asking
the price, decides to take one of the articles brought out
for his inspection in a shop. "Yes, it's very nice," he
said contentedly, then glanced contemplatively at the other
things. "You can give those walking shoes to Fritz in the
Salsergasse, he needs a pair, and the shirts to Josef in the
square, he can patch them for himself. If you like I'll
{uttered} take them along for you." This again he {said} in the
magnanimous tone of one volunteering to do a favour, and I
almost had a feeling that I ought to thank him for distributing
my belongings among people who were complete strangers to me.
Carefully tying up the things into a bundle, he lifted them
on to his shoulder. Then he added in tones of kindly
condescension, "Yes, you're a good fellow. Nice of you to
give all these things away," and vanished.

Strange, no enthusiastic review of any of my books had
ever delighted me so much as {this} naive praise. I have
often thought of Anton, and always with gratitude, for few
people have given me so much spiritual help. Frequently
when I have been worrying about stupid little money matters
I have called to mind this man who lived calmly and confidently
for the day, because he wanted no more than was enough for
that one day. How often have I not seen him in his shabby
clothes, but never otherwise than serene and carefree! And
always I have thought to myself: if everyone in the world
were to learn this secret of mutual trust and confidence,
there would be no police, no courts of law, no prisons and
no money. Our entire complicated economic system, which
reduces itself ever and again to an absurdity and has become
blank

--9--
a "social problem", would perhaps be remedied if everyone
lived like this one man, who only took what he
needed and gave as much of himself as he could. For some
years now I have heard nothing of him. But there are few
people about whom I feel less anxiety: I know that God
will never leave this man in the lurch and - what's more -
men will not, either.

pages.transcription:
Memorandum für Beverly Baxter
4. I 40

As an Austrian who since his childhood has been
familiar with the relations between the former Austria and
Italy, I would like to draw the attention to an actual point
in the present political constellation.

The relations between the old Austria and Italy
have always been insincere owing to the demand of Italy for
"Trento e Trieste", the country of Trentino and the port of
Trieste. A large group in the Austrian Ministeries was
willing, for the sake of the security of prewar Austria,
to sacrifice the Trentino, but all parties were united in
the strict refusal to give up Trieste. Trieste was the
harbour of the fifty million central-European Empire of
Austria, and it was essential for its commerce in the
Mediterranean and with the East.

Now, that Germany has taken over Austria and
Czechoslovakia, there can be no doubt that sooner or later
they will ask for this port which belonged to the old
Austria, and for their commerce an outlet to the Medi-
terranean is absolutely necessary for a country of a
hundred million inhabitants. It is even a hundred times
more important than Danzig, and Germany has only deferred
this demand according to its principle always to attack
pages.notes: Handschriftliche Notiz am Kopf der Seite.

pages.transcription:
- 2 -

the weakest point first. But one day - and especially
if Germany should come out of this war with some strength -
it will and must make this demand for Trieste.

This fact is, of course, well known in Italy.
It is a mistake that the newspapers always point out that
Italy is afraid of the preponderance of Germany in Hungary
or on the Balkan. The real fear in the informed circles is
that a Germany which reaches from the North Sea until a few
hundred miles from the Adriatic, will force its way through
to the Adriatic Sea. Trieste is the sceleton in the house
of the German-Italian relations not less - and even more -
than it was the sceleton in the time of the Triple Alliance
between Austria and Italy.

That this is true is proved by the fact that never
the word Trieste has been mentioned in the German-Italian
relations. No one in Italy talks about this possibility
and yet everybody in responsible circles is strongly con-
cerned about it. It seems, therefore, of utmost importance
that just now this word is brought into the limelight of
discussion and in a way that it cannot be suppressed.
A mere mentioning of the problem of Trieste in newspaper
articles would be without value, for these articles could
easily be suppressed by the German and Italian censorship.
This undeniable fact of the German aspiration for Trieste

- 3 -

which is only deferred, should be pointed out in such a
way that its discussion cannot be suppressed, for instance
in a speech of the leading statesmen such as Chamberlain
or Lord Halifax which go all over the world and cannot be
suppressed.

I do not overlook, of course, that such a
mentioning must be made in such a way that it does not
seem to be made in the intention of driving a wedge
between Germany and Italy. It should, therefore, not only
be spoken of Trieste but in connection with all other
possible demands which a victorious Germany would raise
with the argumentation of the necessary "Lebensraum".
It could for instance be said that England, as long as
Germany did only ask for the return of the German mino-
rities in other countries, has always shown under-
standing for this wish of Germany and has not resisted
it in the hope that in this way a peaceful reorganisation
of Europe would be furthered. But hardly had Germany
reached its aim when it produced a new thesis, that of
the "living space" which in advance justifies every act
of agression. Which demands can be contained in this
elastic word "Lebensraum", is widely known by the
writings of the "Alldeutschen Verband", by the theory

- 4 -

of geopolitics of professor Haushofer and by the maps
which are again and again drawn in Germany since 1914.
One day the Dutch mouth of the Rhine should be given up
to Germany because the Rhine is a German stream and
Holland therefore belongs to the German "Lebensraum",
another time it will have to be Trieste, the harbour of
old Austria, which should be returned to Germany because
Germany has the right to all former Austrian territory -
and because Trieste is indispensable as the accesion to
the Mediterranean for its trade with the Orient. Just
as well the Black Sea belongs to its sphere of domination
because the Danube has its origin in Germany and Austria.
The Stebenbürger Sachsen will have to be freed from the
yoke of Hungary or Rumania etcetera, etcetera. That the
whole of Europa belongs to the sphere of the German "Le-
bensraum", in this theory all the theoreticians of National
Socialism are united, and their discussions only refer to
which aim - either the Mediterranean, the North Sea, the
Mouth of the Rhine, the Balkan - should be attacked first.

I only give these instances as one of the many
possibilities how the word Trieste and the German demand
for this former German harbour could publicly be mentioned.
Perhaps there is a better way of doing this. But in any

- 5 -

case I am convinced that it is necessary to bring Trieste
into the discussion just in the present moment. It is
quite likely that any such intention will immediately be
denied by Germany, but such a dementi would only serve
further to draw the attention of the wide public in Italy
to this problem. Tendencious lies have no strength.
But what would give its effect to a discussion about
Trieste is the fact that in reality Trieste is an actual
and even necessary object in the German Imperialism,
and the reason for a future inevitable conflict between
Italy, which cannot tolerate Germany in the Mediterranean
and Germany which will and must gain access to the Me-
diterranean

{nur gelebt}
Goethe hat die Erde gekannt auf der er wandelte und ging, er hat sie
gekannt im genauesten Sinn des Geologen, des Mineralogen, er hat sie
durchforscht und durchackert und ebenso kannte er die Menschen, in
deren Kreis er lebte, er kannte sie physisch und psychologisch, er
kannte alle Formen ihrer Gemeinschaft und den Sinn ihrer höheren
Ordnungen, man Staat, Gesetz und Kultur nennt. Immer war ihm die
sinnlich gegenwärtige Anschauung jedes Dinges unbedingt notwendig um
sein Geistiges und Gesetzmässiges zu erfassen und je tiefer er ein-
drang in die sinnliche Welt, jemahr der Forscher, der Sammler, der
Gelehrte an realer Kenntnis zunahm, umso höher entwickelte sich sei-
ne dichterische Schau. Gerade das Singuläre zog ihn an. Er
sichte die Handschrift, das Bildnis jedes bedeutenderen
Menschen - seine Lebensspur wie er sagte. Nur Lebensspuren
waren dann auch seine Gedichte, seine Dramen, seine Dar-
stellungen: fast alles, was er geschrieben, hat Bezug auf
tatsächlichen Anlass nur ist ursächlich eingewurzelt in die
reale Welt. Ein Goethe als gleichbleibender als
nicht Wahsender, nicht Saft nur Mark der
tatsächlichen Lebens an sich ziehender, ein Goethe
der auch nur für einen Augenblick auf die
Communication, die Nahrung vom Tagleben ver-
zichtet, er wäre so unmöglich wie ein Baum ohne
Wurzeln - er braucht den Zustrom von Wirkli-
chem von Tatsachen für seine Poesie ebenso
wie den Atem der Luft für die Lunge. Er muss
ja wachsen, weltweit werden, aufsteigen in die
Jahre der Erkenntnis - darum braucht er Bereicherung.
{darum arbeitet er wie ein Taglöhner die}
{abertausend Tage seiner Jahrhunderte}
